title: A micro-review and one thing I love about...The True OSR - Obsolete #$%^^# Rules
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VlgtaUj_dho

A hybrid video. Daniel (@dungeondive) micro-reviews **The True OSR** — a deliberately tongue-in-cheek parody RPG — and lands on a mixed-to-negative verdict on the *system*, paired with full-throated love for one *specific chapter*.

The system, Daniel argues, is less OSR than NSR (New School Rules) — Mörk-Borg-styled in layout and tone, but built around a *deliberately adversarial* GM/player relationship as a design choice. The GM accumulates XP alongside the players; on enough XP, the GM can manifest in-fiction as an Ultra Avatar boss and actively try to kill the party. Both sides have *Golden Rules* that frame the other as the antagonist. 100 character classes. Four levels (low, medium, high, Epic). Genre-busting world-hop between fantasy, sci-fi, post-apocalyptic, horror, and superhero settings. The adversarial frame, in Daniel's reading, will be a love-it-or-hate-it for any given GM.

The chapter he loves outright is **Chapter 5 — the tables**. Over forty d100 random tables cover achievements, animals, archetypes, armour, buildings, clothes, curses, deaths, divinities, dungeons, encounters, food, guilds, heroic attacks, implants, locations, magic items, mutations, plants, poisons, psyche, quests, siege weapons, species, spells, traps, treasures, twists, vehicles, weapons, and *worlds*. Each table cross-references the others. His recommendation: grab the PDF for the tables, ignore the system if the adversarial frame isn't for you.

----

*The True OSR* makes the GM-vs-players relationship explicitly adversarial as a design choice — both sides have rule-of-thumb antagonism written into the book. Do you think a game can productively *design* its way to that dynamic, or does it only work emergently?
